summary: Yawm al-Qiyama, the Day of Resurrection, is the Islamic doctrine of the final day on which the dead are raised and all human beings are brought before God for the reckoning. Belief in the last day is a core article of Islamic faith.
description: Al-qiyama ("the rising" or "resurrection"), most often invoked as Yawm al-Qiyama, the Day of Resurrection, names the culminating day of the eschatological drama, on which the dead are restored to life and gathered for judgment. It is enumerated among the foundational articles of Islamic belief and is a dominant theme of the Qur'an, which describes it under many names, including the Hour (al-saʿa), the Day of Reckoning, and the Last Day. The classical picture, drawn from the Qur'an and hadith and systematized by the theological schools, unfolds in stages: the blowing of the trumpet and the end of the world, the raising and gathering of all creatures, the presentation of each person's record of deeds, the weighing of those deeds in the balance (mizan), and the consignment to paradise (janna) or hell (jahannam). Kalam took up contested questions surrounding these events, including whether the eschatological descriptions are to be read literally or figuratively and how the reckoning is to be reconciled with divine justice and predestination. Al-qiyama is inseparable from maʿad, the return and resurrection, and from the wider doctrine of al-akhira, the hereafter; the day is the occasion on which the return to God is accomplished. The schools differed in emphasis and detail while sharing the affirmation of a real day of resurrection and reckoning.
editorial_notes: This entry treats qiyama as the Day of Resurrection and Judgment, distinguished from but tightly linked to maʿad (the return and resurrection as a doctrine) and nested within al-akhira (eschatology as a whole); all three are cross-linked. The stages of the day (trumpet, gathering, the record, the balance) are described as the classical narrative attributed to the Qur'an, hadith, and the schools, not asserted as fact, and the literal-versus-figurative reading is named as a live dispute without adjudication, per neutrality principles #1 and #2. Three lenses are triangulated (principle #8): a Western academic monograph (Smith and Haddad, The Islamic Understanding of Death and Resurrection); the tradition's own classical primary texts; and a modern Muslim scholar (Fazlur Rahman, Major Themes of the Qur'an, eschatology chapter). Sourcing follow-up (2026-07-19, peer-review gap): the peer reviewer noted that al-Ghazali's Tahafut al-Falasifa supports only the resurrection dispute (bodily resurrection against the philosophers), not the staged Day-of-Judgment sequence (trumpet, gathering, record, mizan) that the entry describes. A classical primary that does carry that sequence has been added, al-Ghazali's al-Durra al-fakhira (The Precious Pearl, trans. Jane Idleman Smith, Scholars Press, 1979), an eschatological treatise narrating the events of the afterlife from death through resurrection to the reckoning (web-verified: title, translator, publisher, year, and its Day-of-Resurrection content confirmed). The Tahafut is retained but re-scoped to the resurrection dispute only. Confidence is honest: Smith and Haddad carries medium confidence for the eschatological narrative; the primary texts and Rahman are cited at medium confidence as their relevant passages were not read line-by-line here. The signs of the Hour, the intercession (shafaʿa), and the bridge (sirat) are deferred to future entries. VERIFIED (2026-07-19): the load-bearing Qur'anic verses (Q 75:1-6, 22:7, 36:78-79) were read directly at quran.com and confirmed to carry the entry's core (the certain Hour, the raising of the dead, the bodily resurrection); a Qur'an source is added at high, and the entry is promoted to verified. The staged sequence and the kalam literal-versus-figurative debate remain attributed at medium (their sources not read line by line).

relations.note: The Day of Resurrection is the central event of al-akhira, the hereafter.
Qiyama is the day on which the return and resurrection (maʿad) are accomplished.
The reckoning and the weighing of deeds enact divine justice.
Reconciling the judgment of the day with predestination is a classical eschatological question.
The day belongs to al-akhira, defined in contrast to al-dunya, the present world.

historical_period.note: A dominant theme of Qur'anic revelation; the sequence of the day was systematized from the Qur'an and hadith by the theological schools from roughly the 8th century CE onward.

sources.citation: The Qur'an, passages on the Day of Resurrection: Surah al-Qiyama (Q 75:1-6, the oath by the Day of Resurrection and the reassembly of the bones and fingertips), Q 22:7 ('the Hour is coming, there is no doubt about it, and Allah will resurrect those in the graves'), and Q 36:78-79 (reviving decayed bones). The primary scriptural basis for the bodily resurrection and the reckoning.
sources.license: Primary religious text; classical Arabic in the public domain. Verses read directly (2026-07-19) and confirmed.

sources.citation: Smith, Jane Idleman, and Yvonne Yazbeck Haddad. The Islamic Understanding of Death and Resurrection. Albany: State University of New York Press, 1981; repr. Oxford University Press, 2002. An academic monograph reconstructing the Islamic eschatological narrative from the Qur'an, hadith, creeds, and theological commentaries, including the events of the Day of Resurrection (yawm al-qiyama): the raising and gathering, the reckoning, the weighing of deeds, and the final abodes.

al-Ghazali, Abu Hamid. The Precious Pearl (Kitab al-Durra al-fakhira fi kashf ʿulum al-akhira), trans. Jane Idleman Smith. Missoula, MT: Scholars Press, 1979. An eschatological treatise (attributed to al-Ghazali) that narrates the events of the afterlife in sequence, from death through the barzakh to the Day of Resurrection: the gathering of the dead, the presentation of the record of deeds, and the reckoning. Added as the classical primary text carrying the staged Day-of-Judgment narrative the entry describes; cited at medium confidence as its relevant passages were not read line-by-line here.

al-Ghazali, Abu Hamid. The Incoherence of the Philosophers (Tahafut al-Falasifa): A Parallel English-Arabic Text, trans. Michael E. Marmura, 2nd ed. Provo, UT: Brigham Young University Press, 2000. In its treatment of the afterlife, al-Ghazali affirms the bodily resurrection of the dead against the philosophers. Retained specifically for the resurrection dispute (whether the dead are raised in body), not for the staged sequence of the Day, which the Durra al-fakhira now carries. Cited as a classical primary text at medium confidence as its relevant passages were not read line-by-line here.

Rahman, Fazlur. Major Themes of the Qur'an. Minneapolis and Chicago: Bibliotheca Islamica, 1980; 2nd ed. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1994 (repr. 2009). The chapter on eschatology by a leading modern Muslim scholar treats the Day of Resurrection and Judgment as a central and recurring theme of the Qur'an, tied to human accountability. Added as the modern Muslim scholarly lens; cited at medium confidence as its relevant passages were not read line-by-line here.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.
